item.title: This one belongs up there with turn signal fluid. Have a good week!
item.description: I can see the Grinch grin, growing on the shopkeeper now. EDIT - Most Viral? Thank you my Dudes! May The Force Be With You! EDIT EDIT - Thank you so much for the funny anecdotes in your replies. I'm really enjoying the creativity I'm reading from you Dudes.

item.title: Russian draft in Luhansk
item.description: A bunch of Russians going door-to-door in Luhansk issuing draft notices. They ask where they lady’s son is. She says that the he had left and he was not at home. The soldier then asked "why are you filming?" The lady replies: “for a trial in The Hague".

item.title: Progress!!
item.description: My mom sent me the first picture from 10 years ago when I was super depressed and eating my feelings ? 60lbs down and now able to deadlift more than twice my body weight! It’s crazy what taking care of your physical and mental health can do for you

item.title: Random Sunday Dumparoo
item.description:
Gross when you really think about it
Hahaha
I’ve done this before…
Sound on
That’s crazzzzy … would you like a bag today?
Pet snuggles
The little French fry
2006 was a wild year
Need another vacay, I’d only sleep when traveling with people though, never alone!
Bubble butt, bubble bubble bubble butt.
Git to the Walmart ASAP
HA
Especially if a 12 year old is taking your order and grandma and gramps are cooking in the back!
Mmm gumbo
I used to drive by bucees everyday to and from work and never stopped. One day I’ll go!
Hmmm
Always leave forgetting the one thing I went for too.
Awkwaaard
Is it weird I want to spend 2/3 hours plucking his eyebrows and shaping them?
20/20 over here
I want this shirt
Puppy tax, Luna fell asleep in the car, full on snoring, I tried moving her and she went back into this position by flopping backwards.

Man's thicc

:)
On one hand, an amazing bouquet of crystal. On the other, a d6/d8 bludgeoning weapon
Or a faerie circle, either works
Floofy Demon tax
item.dc:creator: Shamarr

item.title: At least I told them
item.description: In a long term marriage. Talked with my wife about everything and we both come to realize I'm gay. She was 100% on the same page, supporting me and agreed to stay close friends. Told my parents and their response was how they were heartbroken, don't believe it's true and couldn't believe how I had chosen to destroy my marriage. My mom could not understand that my wife was not totally devastated and didn't hate me. Family basically made it all about them and how they were so hurt by my "choice". I expected they wouldn't approve, but it hurts way more than I thought it would - especially that they didn't ever consider or ask how I was doing. I know I'm going to be OK, but it sucks a lot. Long Edit for clarity/context: My wife and I have been together for 16 years and have a kid together. I was raised religious and was very much anti-gay for a long time. Eventually, I realized how much hate it created in me and how much pain it caused others to live that way and I had to change. I never truly considered that I might be gay because by the time I was really no longer anti-gay, I was already married. I had come a long way by that point, but still had a very long way to go. When I really let myself explore the idea, I realized I was probably bi. Had that conversation with my wife and as we did, we both were like, actually, maybe I'm more than just bi. Explored it with her permission with a guy and it was like a switch got flipped and I was like 'oh, apparently I'm very gay'. My parents aren't terrible people, but they do, like everyone does, have blind spots. I don't hate them, and I do believe they've grown a lot since I was a kid, but they still got more growing to do. We've always been pretty close, but over the last few years, we've drifted apart as I've become far more liberal and they've become far more conservative. I've also had a ton of support from friends, co-workers and strangers on the internet. Which has been amazing. I just wish I could get support from the family I really care about. I'm doing better, but it still hurts a lot and I don't know if I'll ever be OK with them after this - even if they do accept me eventually.
item.dc:creator: wontondisregard

item.title: Dispelling Chornobyl myths that Imgur is buying into lately, hard
item.description:
Dytiatky Checkpoint near Ivankiv, before the invasion and today after Russian troops left. This is the southermost checkpoint of the outer 30km boundary of the Exclusion Zone, closest to Kyiv. This is where visitors, vehicles from the capital, and tourists enter the Zone.
Opposite view, looking toward Kyiv. The smashed yellow wooden hut is a souvenir stand, the yellow hut on the right is a coffee stand that sells drinks to people waiting in line to enter the Zone on busy days. Some visitors arrive in cars, but most in 10-passenger vans or even 50-passenger buses; it can take a while to check all documents before entering.
So I've seen two things on Imgur a lot lately. One is the belief that visiting the Zone is deadly, or extremely dangerous. To put it in plain terms, the radiation exposure a person would receive when standing directly in front of the power plant for an hour today is equivalent to the dose an airline pilot gets for every hour on the job. There are places in the Zone, certain rooftops and wildland, that were never fully decontaminated, and would be unwise to visit, but the reality is that the halflife of the most radioactive materials has already passed in the 30 years since the accident of 1986. It should be known that, had you wished, before the invasion it was possible for tourists to visit the original Control Room 4, and inside the New Safe Confinement, and even to go see the original Joker cleanup robot in the high level waste disposal yard.
I've seen imgurians respond to the above photo with absolute panic. Verbatim "Don’t stay there. Do NOT stay there. There’s radiation all over the place, you’re going to get sick! Get the fuck out of there!" It should be noted that in addition to tour guides, there are about 2,000 people that live and work full time in the Zone. Either at the power plant itself, radiological research labs in the area, or in the village of Chornobyl itself.
This is a video of where "The Red Forest" currently stands. Important to note that all the original trees were pushed over and buried, and any trees currently standing are new growth since 1986. Radiation is higher here, and higher than anywhere in the Zone, but only about double what you'd get outside the plant (remember what I said about airline pilots?) And lastly, regarding the reports of Russians withdrawing and experience ARS (acute radiation sickness) - Well, here are the thoughts on that as written by the Head of the Radioecological Monitoring Group at Chornobyl Radioecological Center: Let's talk about the viral hit of recent days - about the trenches in the Red Forest and the crowds of armed Russian soldiers in Gomel. In the midst of Chernobyl pros, this story was met with a slight confusion. Александр Купный responded first, then Chornobyl insight begin with the facts. The first - so far no one has seen the trenches in the Red Forest. The head of the Zone Management Agency, referring to Chernobyl personnel, said that the occupants built a checkpoint behind the "torch". The trenches were seen at the turn to Shepelichi. The second - there is no official report about the radiation cases. Do you seriously believe a country that hides its losses from the public would pull off such an epic fail. What is there? The invaders were definitely in the zone all this time and kept the Chernobyl under control, stopping in Chernobyl. There is one photo from the Republican Scientific Center for Radiation Medicine and Human Ecology in the city. Gomel, where medical vans of the Russian army stand near the main corps. That's what this story was designed from. Going into the details: So, the Red Forest is a conditional territory of 10 km. square west of the Chernobyl NPP, where between 1986-1987 middle-aged pine plantations died as a result of dose overload. This is the most polluted part of the Zone and one of the most valuable polygons in terms of studying isotope migration and radiation effects. But it's definitely not a deadly place. During the period since 1986, the power of the exposure dose here has decreased by 2-3 orders. Yes, to work in "Ryzhik", as we say, you need a dose outfit. The power of the exposure dose reaches 2-4 milli-rays per hour here. It's a lot, but as the character of one movie said - "Not Great, Not Terrible" - is permissible for experts. Our and foreign researchers actively worked in the Red Forest for many hours a day. Sometimes they dug soil profiles, I didn't have to. After such work, no one fell ill with radiation and did not go to recovery. This is to be expected - there is no way radiation doses can cause radiation illness in both acute and chronic form. For deterministic effects, one needs to live in the Red Forest for a couple of years, no less. The next question is what did the medical transport of the Russian Armed Forces do at the Republican Scientific and Practical Center for Radiation Medicine and Human Ecology. Probably, he brought the wounded to the hospital, where there are 360 beds, or they examined the personal composition for radionuclide content (HIS) in the clinic. Despite its name, the center is not something special, where retrained patients are treated. This is a regular medical institution aimed at the population of victims of the Chernobyl accident. The issue of radioactive dust, which causes death, remains. Again, we do not have evidence that the equipment was in the Red Forest. In the only video we see heavy traffic on the route "Benevka-Chernobyl-Lelev". I assume the area was used as a transit area. Therefore, the removal will not differ much from what was in the usual times when many civilian transport moved through the area. According to the Chernobyl Director, during the exit of the troops of the aggressor exceeding the radiation background on the station site was not noted. We do not know, but we can assume that on the exit the equipment could have undergone radiation-dosymetric control in the Polessky Reserve or by the forces of the RBHZ units. I wasn't surprised by this story because I've always said the Zone was a space of myth. Some of my friends used to say, "it's ok - it's a cool story that will scare the enemy away." I don't agree with this: it's a dangerous way to underestimate the enemy, to make a caricature out of him in accordance with the wishes of the public. It is important to be realistic and for another reason - the Russian-Ukrainian war is now being interpreted as something more than protecting one country from the attack of another. This is a conflict of worldviews, which is called in different ways: "the struggle of the fucking with the fucking" (Kazantsev), the struggle of civilization and barbarism, or the frontier of Europe (the West). If we take on the side of Western civilization, we must go to the end, accept its categories, values and principles, including truth, rationalism and the scientific method. P.S. On March 31 and April 1, information began to come that Russian forces were leaving the exclusion zone. Over the past two days, I have received several requests from volunteers for our fighters who will carry out missions in the Zone. The original message was only one - there is radiation and no one wants to get a radiation disease. That's why you need to get the dosimeter. I explained as best I could that an unprepared person with a dosimeter is not effective and in most locations it is not needed. Then it got cooler - they asked for iodine and laminaria in capsules... What's next - to give a lecture on how to effectively protect yourself from mutant attacks? Or will the recipes from the times of Liquidation about the radio-protective effects of alcohol and red wine emerge? I can also advise you to buy chocolate bars - veterans of Soviet nuclear polygons defended their testicles with foil against them during the tests. It turned out to be a good meme, funny, got a lot of likes and reposts. The other side of it - how well will they clean the Special Area if they will constantly keep the fear of radiation and radiation disease in their head? We will learn about this in the future - by the number of missed mines and ammunition.
So there it is. Straight from the people that live and work in the Zone. TL;DR: Zone is not scary, probably can't kill you. Could make you sick, but there is no direct evidence of any Russian troops actually suffering from ARS. Lots of memes and assumptions that undermine reality, though. Remember that Russia doesn't admit its losses and wouldn't admit something as embarrassing as this.
item.dc:creator: AtomicAerials
